1:
"At that time the LORD said unto me, Hew thee two tables of stone like unto the first, and come up unto me into the mount, and make thee an ark of wood." The witnesses note God bidding Moses hew two new tables and make an ark, after the first tables were broken. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was at the end of the second forty days, after Moses had fallen down before the LORD for the people's sin of the calf, not the first when the first tables were given and broken. The witnesses note that he was to hew the tables and make an ark. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the hew two tables — new tables for the broken; the come up unto me — to receive the law again; the after the calf — the covenant breached and restored; the grace over the breach — god renewing the covenant in mercy.

The witnesses agree the renewing of the tables is an act of pure grace after the breach of the calf: though Israel had broken the covenant and Moses the tables, God commanded new ones to be hewn and the law given again — the covenant restored where it had been shattered; the second tables are a standing witness that God does not cast off his people for their sin but renews his covenant in mercy, the law given afresh to a people forgiven, grace triumphing over the breach.

So the witnesses make the verse the second tables: The witnesses note the hew two tables: new tables for the broken. The witnesses note the come up unto me: to receive the law again. The witnesses note the after the calf: the covenant breached and restored. The witnesses note the grace over the breach: God renewing the covenant in mercy. The expositors observe the hew two tables: new tables for the broken. The expositors observe the come up unto me: to receive the law again. The expositors observe the after the calf: the covenant breached and restored. The expositors observe the grace over the breach: God renewing the covenant in mercy. The believer is taught to mark the hew two tables, the come up unto me, the after the calf, the grace over the breach, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that God bade Moses hew new tables after the calf — the covenant restored where it had been shattered, grace triumphing over the breach, the law given afresh to a forgiven people.
2:
"And I will write on the tables the words that were in the first tables which thou brakest, and thou shalt put them in the ark." The witnesses note God himself writing the same words again, to be laid up in the ark. The witnesses note that Gill notes that, though hewn by Moses, the writing was the Lord's; the very same laws, in the same words without variation, to show their authenticity, that they were of God and not Moses. The witnesses note that they were to be put in the ark. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the I will write — the writing God's own; the the same words — unchanged from the first; the of God, not Moses — the law's authenticity proved; the put in the ark — kept under the mercy-seat (Heb. 9:4).

The witnesses agree the divine writing marks the law as wholly God's and unchanged: the words on the second tables were the same as the first, written by God's own hand without alteration, proving the law no invention of Moses but the abiding will of God; and their being laid up in the ark — beneath the mercy-seat — shows the law preserved at the heart of the sanctuary, the unchanging commandment kept under the place of atonement, a figure of how the law is honoured and fulfilled in the covenant of grace (cf. Heb. 9:4).

So the witnesses make the verse the law rewritten: The witnesses note the I will write: the writing God's own. The witnesses note the the same words: unchanged from the first. The witnesses note the of God, not Moses: the law's authenticity proved. The witnesses note the put in the ark: kept under the mercy-seat (Heb. 9:4). The expositors observe the I will write: the writing God's own. The expositors observe the the same words: unchanged from the first. The expositors observe the of God, not Moses: the law's authenticity proved. The expositors observe the put in the ark: kept under the mercy-seat (Heb. 9:4). The believer is taught to mark the I will write, the the same words, the of God, not Moses, the put in the ark, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that God wrote the same words again, to be put in the ark — the law wholly his and unchanged, laid up under the mercy-seat, honoured at the heart of the sanctuary (Heb. 9:4).
3:
"And I made an ark of shittim wood, and hewed two tables of stone like unto the first, and went up into the mount, having the two tables in mine hand." The witnesses note Moses making the ark of shittim wood and hewing the tables as commanded. The witnesses note that Gill notes the ark was made (ordered to be made by Bezaleel) of shittim wood, the same as the ark in the holy of holies. The witnesses note that Clarke refers to the shittim wood (Exod. 25:5). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the made an ark — of shittim wood; the hewed the tables — as the first; the went up into the mount — to receive the law; the a type of Christ — the law treasured in him.

The witnesses agree Moses' obedience exactly answered the command: he made the ark of shittim wood and hewed the tables, and went up to receive the law again; the careful doing of all God appointed shows the readiness of the servant to fulfil his Lord's word in every detail, and the ark of incorruptible wood, made to hold the law, stands as a quiet type — the law treasured in a vessel that pictures Christ, in whom the commandment is perfectly kept and preserved.

So the witnesses make the verse the ark made: The witnesses note the made an ark: of shittim wood. The witnesses note the hewed the tables: as the first. The witnesses note the went up into the mount: to receive the law. The witnesses note the a type of Christ: the law treasured in him. The expositors observe the made an ark: of shittim wood. The expositors observe the hewed the tables: as the first. The expositors observe the went up into the mount: to receive the law. The expositors observe the a type of Christ: the law treasured in him. The believer is taught to mark the made an ark, the hewed the tables, the went up into the mount, the a type of Christ, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Moses made the ark and hewed the tables as commanded — the servant's exact obedience, the ark of incorruptible wood a quiet type of Christ, in whom the law is kept and treasured.
4:
"And he wrote on the tables, according to the first writing, the ten commandments... and the LORD gave them unto me." The witnesses note God writing the ten commandments again, identical to the first. The witnesses note that Gill notes the same laws, in the same letters. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the words on the second tables were the same as on the first — the ten commandments. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he wrote — god's own hand again; the according to the first — the same letters; the the ten commandments — the moral law unchanged; the neither added nor diminished — word for word as before; the fixed and perpetual — the standard unaltered by men's failures.

The witnesses agree the verse fixes the unchanging identity of the law: the second tables bore "the ten commandments," word for word and letter for letter as the first, the moral law standing immutable through the breach and the renewal; the ten words, the sum of man's duty to God and neighbour, abide the same in every age, neither added to nor diminished — a witness that God's moral law is fixed and perpetual, the standard of righteousness unaltered by the failures of men.

So the witnesses make the verse the ten commandments: The witnesses note the he wrote: God's own hand again. The witnesses note the according to the first: the same letters. The witnesses note the the ten commandments: the moral law unchanged. The witnesses note the neither added nor diminished: word for word as before. The witnesses note the fixed and perpetual: the standard unaltered by men's failures. The expositors observe the he wrote: God's own hand again. The expositors observe the according to the first: the same letters. The expositors observe the the ten commandments: the moral law unchanged. The expositors observe the neither added nor diminished: word for word as before. The expositors observe the fixed and perpetual: the standard unaltered by men's failures. The believer is taught to mark the he wrote, the according to the first, the the ten commandments, the neither added nor diminished, the fixed and perpetual, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that God wrote the ten commandments again, identical to the first — the moral law immutable through the breach, fixed and perpetual, the standard of righteousness unaltered.
5:
"And I turned myself and came down from the mount, and put the tables in the ark which I had made; and there they be, as the LORD commanded me." The witnesses note Moses placing the tables in the ark, where they remained as God commanded. The witnesses note that Gill notes he turned from the Lord, out of whose hands he had received the tables. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads "there they be" as "they continued there"; the ark in which they ultimately remained was made afterward by Bezaleel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the came down — from the mount; the put the tables in the ark — the law laid up; the there they be — abiding through the generations; the law and mercy — meeting under the mercy-seat.

The witnesses agree the laying-up of the tables in the ark, where "there they be," marks the law's settled and abiding place in the covenant: the commandments were preserved in the holiest vessel, kept safe through all the generations; whatever the order of the making of the permanent ark, the point stands that God's law was treasured at the centre of his sanctuary, abiding under the mercy-seat — the abiding word of God kept, as it were, beneath the place of atonement, law and mercy meeting in the ark.

So the witnesses make the verse the tables kept: The witnesses note the came down: from the mount. The witnesses note the put the tables in the ark: the law laid up. The witnesses note the there they be: abiding through the generations. The witnesses note the law and mercy: meeting under the mercy-seat. The expositors observe the came down: from the mount. The expositors observe the put the tables in the ark: the law laid up. The expositors observe the there they be: abiding through the generations. The expositors observe the law and mercy: meeting under the mercy-seat. The believer is taught to mark the came down, the put the tables in the ark, the there they be, the law and mercy, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Moses put the tables in the ark, where they remained — the law's settled and abiding place, treasured under the mercy-seat, law and mercy meeting in the ark.
6:
"And the children of Israel took their journey from Beeroth of the children of Jaakan to Mosera: there Aaron died, and there he was buried; and Eleazar his son ministered in the priest's office in his stead." The witnesses note the journey, the death of Aaron, and the succession of Eleazar in the priesthood. The witnesses note that Gill notes the difficulty of the route, Benejaakan, and the account of Aaron's death and burial. The witnesses note that Eleazar his son ministered in the priest's office in his stead. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Aaron died — the first high priest gone; the there he was buried — his course ended; the Eleazar ministered — succeeding in the office; the the priesthood abides — pointing to the priest who continueth ever (Heb. 7:24).

The witnesses agree the verse marks the passing of the first high priest and the unbroken continuance of the priestly office: Aaron died and was buried, yet Eleazar at once "ministered in his stead," the office living on though the man was gone; the smooth succession shows that God's appointed ministry does not fail with the death of his servants but is carried on, a single priest passing yet the priesthood abiding — pointing beyond the changing line of mortal priests to the one who "continueth ever" with an unchangeable priesthood (cf. Heb. 7:24).

So the witnesses make the verse Aaron dies, Eleazar succeeds: The witnesses note the Aaron died: the first high priest gone. The witnesses note the there he was buried: his course ended. The witnesses note the Eleazar ministered: succeeding in the office. The witnesses note the the priesthood abides: pointing to the priest who continueth ever (Heb. 7:24). The expositors observe the Aaron died: the first high priest gone. The expositors observe the there he was buried: his course ended. The expositors observe the Eleazar ministered: succeeding in the office. The expositors observe the the priesthood abides: pointing to the priest who continueth ever (Heb. 7:24). The believer is taught to mark the Aaron died, the there he was buried, the Eleazar ministered, the the priesthood abides, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Aaron died and Eleazar ministered in his stead — the office living on though the man was gone, the changing line of mortal priests pointing to the priest who continueth ever (Heb. 7:24).
7:
"From thence they journeyed unto Gudgodah; and from Gudgodah to Jotbath, a land of rivers of waters." The witnesses note the continuing stages of the journey, to Gudgodah and Jotbath, a land of waters. The witnesses note that Gill notes Jarchi takes Gudgodah to be the same as Horhagidgad, while Aben Ezra reads it as a general name including several stations after Mount Hor. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch continue the list of journeys. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the to Gudgodah — a stage of the march; the to Jotbath — a land of rivers of waters; the God's continued leading — from place to place; the refreshment in the desert — a well-watered resting-place.

The witnesses agree the bare itinerary serves a quiet purpose in the discourse: the naming of the stages of the march — to Gudgodah, to Jotbath "a land of rivers of waters" — records God's continued leading of his people from place to place, even to a well-watered resting-place in the desert; the mention of the land of waters amid the dry journey is a small token of his provision, the God who led them also refreshing them, the wilderness marked by the stations of his faithful guidance.

So the witnesses make the verse the journey continued: The witnesses note the to Gudgodah: a stage of the march. The witnesses note the to Jotbath: a land of rivers of waters. The witnesses note the God's continued leading: from place to place. The witnesses note the refreshment in the desert: a well-watered resting-place. The expositors observe the to Gudgodah: a stage of the march. The expositors observe the to Jotbath: a land of rivers of waters. The expositors observe the God's continued leading: from place to place. The expositors observe the refreshment in the desert: a well-watered resting-place. The believer is taught to mark the to Gudgodah, the to Jotbath, the God's continued leading, the refreshment in the desert, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Israel journeyed to Gudgodah and Jotbath, a land of waters — God's continued leading from stage to stage, refreshing his people even in the desert.
8:
"At that time the LORD separated the tribe of Levi, to bear the ark of the covenant of the LORD, to stand before the LORD to minister unto him, and to bless in his name, unto this day." The witnesses note God's setting apart of Levi to bear the ark, minister, and bless in his name. The witnesses note that Gill notes the separation was after the tabernacle was erected, not at the descent from the mount nor at Aaron's death. The witnesses note that Levi was to bear the ark, stand to minister, and bless in his name. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the separated the tribe — set apart to God; the to bear the ark — carry his presence; the to minister — serve before the LORD; the to bless in his name — speak his benediction (Num. 6:23).

The witnesses agree the threefold office of Levi marks the dignity of the consecrated service: they were set apart to bear the ark, to minister before the LORD, and to bless in his name — to carry his presence, serve at his altar, and speak his benediction over the people; the calling of a whole tribe to the sacred service shows God's provision of a ministry for his worship, a foreshadowing of the church's ministers who serve the Lord and bless his people, set apart wholly to holy things (cf. Num. 6:23).

So the witnesses make the verse Levi separated: The witnesses note the separated the tribe: set apart to God. The witnesses note the to bear the ark: carry his presence. The witnesses note the to minister: serve before the LORD. The witnesses note the to bless in his name: speak his benediction (Num. 6:23). The expositors observe the separated the tribe: set apart to God. The expositors observe the to bear the ark: carry his presence. The expositors observe the to minister: serve before the LORD. The expositors observe the to bless in his name: speak his benediction (Num. 6:23). The believer is taught to mark the separated the tribe, the to bear the ark, the to minister, the to bless in his name, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that God separated Levi to bear the ark, minister, and bless in his name — the dignity of the consecrated service, a ministry for his worship set apart wholly to holy things (Num. 6:23).
9:
"Wherefore Levi hath no part nor inheritance with his brethren; the LORD is his inheritance, according as the LORD thy God promised him." The witnesses note that Levi had no land-inheritance, for the LORD himself was his portion. The witnesses note that Gill notes Levi had no part in the division of Canaan, being separated to the sanctuary's service and having no leisure to plough and sow. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the LORD as his inheritance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no part with his brethren — no land in Canaan; the separated to the service — no time to till the ground; the the LORD is his inheritance — god himself his portion; the the believer's comfort — the LORD the portion of mine inheritance (Ps. 16:5).

The witnesses agree the want of an earthly portion was Levi's highest privilege: having no land, "the LORD is his inheritance" — God himself their portion in place of fields; the exchange teaches that those who give themselves wholly to God's service find their all in him, and the word is the comfort of every believer, who can say "the LORD is the portion of mine inheritance" (Ps. 16:5), counting God himself a better treasure than any earthly possession.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD his inheritance: The witnesses note the no part with his brethren: no land in Canaan. The witnesses note the separated to the service: no time to till the ground. The witnesses note the the LORD is his inheritance: God himself his portion. The witnesses note the the believer's comfort: the LORD the portion of mine inheritance (Ps. 16:5). The expositors observe the no part with his brethren: no land in Canaan. The expositors observe the separated to the service: no time to till the ground. The expositors observe the the LORD is his inheritance: God himself his portion. The expositors observe the the believer's comfort: the LORD the portion of mine inheritance (Ps. 16:5). The believer is taught to mark the no part with his brethren, the separated to the service, the the LORD is his inheritance, the the believer's comfort, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Levi had no land, for the LORD himself was his inheritance — those given wholly to God finding their all in him, the believer's comfort that the LORD is the portion of mine inheritance (Ps. 16:5).
10:
"And I stayed in the mount, according to the first time, forty days and forty nights; and the LORD hearkened unto me at that time also, and the LORD would not destroy thee." The witnesses note Moses' second forty days of intercession, and God's hearkening to spare the people. The witnesses note that Gill notes he stayed and fasted as long as the first time, before coming down with the two tables. The witnesses note that the LORD hearkened to him and would not destroy them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the stayed forty days — the second intercession; the the LORD hearkened — the prayer prevailing; the would not destroy thee — the people spared; the the mediator's pleading — pointing to Christ's availing intercession (Heb. 7:25).

The witnesses agree the verse magnifies the power of intercession and the mercy of God: Moses stayed forty days again, pleading for the people, and "the LORD hearkened" and would not destroy them — the sin of the calf forgiven at the prayer of the mediator; the prevailing of Moses' intercession to turn away wrath shows the worth of a mediator's pleading, and points to the one Mediator whose intercession never fails, by whom the guilty are spared and the covenant kept (cf. Heb. 7:25).

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD hearkened: The witnesses note the stayed forty days: the second intercession. The witnesses note the the LORD hearkened: the prayer prevailing. The witnesses note the would not destroy thee: the people spared. The witnesses note the the mediator's pleading: pointing to Christ's availing intercession (Heb. 7:25). The expositors observe the stayed forty days: the second intercession. The expositors observe the the LORD hearkened: the prayer prevailing. The expositors observe the would not destroy thee: the people spared. The expositors observe the the mediator's pleading: pointing to Christ's availing intercession (Heb. 7:25). The believer is taught to mark the stayed forty days, the the LORD hearkened, the would not destroy thee, the the mediator's pleading, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Moses interceded forty days and the LORD hearkened, sparing the people — the power of the mediator's pleading, pointing to the one Mediator whose intercession never fails (Heb. 7:25).
11:
"And the LORD said unto me, Arise, take thy journey before the people, that they may go in and possess the land, which I sware unto their fathers to give unto them." The witnesses note God bidding Moses lead the people on to possess the land, the sin forgiven. The witnesses note that Gill notes that, upon Moses' supplication, the Lord was graciously pleased not only to forgive but to order him to go before the people, that they might possess the land. The witnesses note that this was the land sworn to their fathers. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the arise, take thy journey — the march resumed; the before the people — moses leading them on; the go in and possess — to their inheritance; the forgiveness sealed — grace restoring them to their purpose.

The witnesses agree the command to advance is the seal of the forgiveness: the same God who had threatened to destroy now bids Moses lead the people on to their inheritance, the journey resumed and the promise renewed; the moving-forward after the pardon shows that God's grace not only forgives sin but restores his people to their purpose, taking up again the fulfilment of his oath — mercy not merely sparing but blessing, the forgiven people sent on to possess the land sworn to the fathers.

So the witnesses make the verse arise and possess: The witnesses note the arise, take thy journey: the march resumed. The witnesses note the before the people: Moses leading them on. The witnesses note the go in and possess: to their inheritance. The witnesses note the forgiveness sealed: grace restoring them to their purpose. The expositors observe the arise, take thy journey: the march resumed. The expositors observe the before the people: Moses leading them on. The expositors observe the go in and possess: to their inheritance. The expositors observe the forgiveness sealed: grace restoring them to their purpose. The believer is taught to mark the arise, take thy journey, the before the people, the go in and possess, the forgiveness sealed, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that God bade Moses lead the people on to possess the land — the seal of forgiveness, grace not merely sparing but restoring the people to their purpose, the promise to the fathers renewed.
12:
"And now, Israel, what doth the LORD thy God require of thee, but to fear the LORD thy God, to walk in all his ways, and to love him, and to serve the LORD thy God with all thy heart and with all thy soul," The witnesses note the great summary of duty — to fear, walk in his ways, love, and serve the LORD with the whole heart. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is asked for all the favours bestowed — the forgiveness of their sins and the renewal of the promise of the land. The witnesses note that Henry marks a most pathetic exhortation to obedience, inferred from all God's dealings. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the what doth the LORD require — the sum of duty asked; the to fear and love him — reverence and affection; the to walk in his ways — obedience of life; the serve with all thy heart — the whole man devoted (Mic. 6:8).

The witnesses agree this verse gathers the whole of religion into a few great words: God requires of his people to fear him, walk in his ways, love him, and serve him with all the heart and soul — reverence, obedience, love, and service in one; the question "what doth the LORD require?" set against all his mercies shows that obedience is the reasonable response of a forgiven and favoured people, the sum of duty being not a burden but the loving devotion of the whole man to the God who has done such great things, a parallel to the prophet's "what doth the LORD require of thee?" (Mic. 6:8).

So the witnesses make the verse what God requires: The witnesses note the what doth the LORD require: the sum of duty asked. The witnesses note the to fear and love him: reverence and affection. The witnesses note the to walk in his ways: obedience of life. The witnesses note the serve with all thy heart: the whole man devoted (Mic. 6:8). The expositors observe the what doth the LORD require: the sum of duty asked. The expositors observe the to fear and love him: reverence and affection. The expositors observe the to walk in his ways: obedience of life. The expositors observe the serve with all thy heart: the whole man devoted (Mic. 6:8). The believer is taught to mark the what doth the LORD require, the to fear and love him, the to walk in his ways, the serve with all thy heart, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that God requires his people to fear, love, and serve him with the whole heart — the sum of religion in a few words, the reasonable devotion of a forgiven people to the God of all their mercies (Mic. 6:8).
13:
"To keep the commandments of the LORD, and his statutes, which I command thee this day for thy good?" The witnesses note that the keeping of God's commandments is required, and that for the people's own good. The witnesses note that Gill notes both the ten commands and all others were to be kept. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the commandments commanded "for thy good." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the keep the commandments — the duty required; the and his statutes — the whole law; the for thy good — the gracious end of it; the obedience and blessing one — the law the way of welfare (Ps. 19:11).

The witnesses agree the verse crowns the summary of duty with the gracious end of it: the keeping of God's commandments is required "for thy good," the law given not to burden but to bless those who keep it; the little phrase overturns every thought of God's commands as harsh or arbitrary, showing that obedience is the way of the creature's own welfare, the Lawgiver seeking in every precept the lasting good of his people — so that to keep his word is to be happy, his commandments and our blessing being one (cf. Ps. 19:11).

So the witnesses make the verse for thy good: The witnesses note the keep the commandments: the duty required. The witnesses note the and his statutes: the whole law. The witnesses note the for thy good: the gracious end of it. The witnesses note the obedience and blessing one: the law the way of welfare (Ps. 19:11). The expositors observe the keep the commandments: the duty required. The expositors observe the and his statutes: the whole law. The expositors observe the for thy good: the gracious end of it. The expositors observe the obedience and blessing one: the law the way of welfare (Ps. 19:11). The believer is taught to mark the keep the commandments, the and his statutes, the for thy good, the obedience and blessing one, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the keeping of the commandments is required for the people's own good — the law given to bless, obedience the way of welfare, the commandments and our blessing being one (Ps. 19:11).
14:
"Behold, the heaven and the heaven of heavens is the LORD's thy God, the earth also, with all that therein is." The witnesses note the greatness of God — owner of the highest heavens and all the earth. The witnesses note that Gill notes the airy and starry heaven, and the third heaven, the heaven of heavens, the seat of the divine Majesty, are the Lord's, made and possessed by him. The witnesses note that the earth also, with all in it, is his. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the heaven of heavens — the highest heaven his; the the earth also — with all its fulness; the maker and owner — of all things; the the ground of grace — the God of all yet loving one (Ps. 24:1).

The witnesses agree the verse magnifies the universal sovereignty of God as the ground of his claim and the wonder of his grace: the heaven of heavens and the earth with all its fulness are his, the maker and owner of all things; against this vast dominion the marvel of the next verse stands out — that the God who possesses all should set his love on one people; the greatness of God here owned both humbles the creature and exalts the grace that, needing nothing, yet chose to love (cf. Ps. 24:1, the earth is the LORD's, and the fulness thereof).

So the witnesses make the verse all is the LORD's: The witnesses note the the heaven of heavens: the highest heaven his. The witnesses note the the earth also: with all its fulness. The witnesses note the maker and owner: of all things. The witnesses note the the ground of grace: the God of all yet loving one (Ps. 24:1). The expositors observe the the heaven of heavens: the highest heaven his. The expositors observe the the earth also: with all its fulness. The expositors observe the maker and owner: of all things. The expositors observe the the ground of grace: the God of all yet loving one (Ps. 24:1). The believer is taught to mark the the heaven of heavens, the the earth also, the maker and owner, the the ground of grace, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the heaven of heavens and the earth are the LORD's — his universal sovereignty, the wonder that the God who possesses all should set his love on one people (Ps. 24:1).
15:
"Only the LORD had a delight in thy fathers to love them, and he chose their seed after them, even you above all people, as it is this day." The witnesses note that God, who owns all, yet set his special love on the fathers and chose their seed. The witnesses note that Gill notes that, though heaven and earth are the Lord's by creation, he had a special regard and peculiar complacency in Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, whence arose his expressions of love. The witnesses note that he chose their seed above all people. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the delight in thy fathers — his special love on them; the to love them — of his own good pleasure; the chose their seed — above all people; the free grace — the God of all singling out the few (Eph. 1:4).

The witnesses agree the verse exalts the freeness of electing love against the backdrop of God's universal dominion: though all peoples are his, he was pleased to "delight in thy fathers to love them" and to choose their seed above all — a love resting on his own good pleasure, not on any claim in them; the marvel is that the God who needed nothing, owning heaven and earth, freely set his heart on one people, the sovereignty of his grace shining the brighter that it singled out the few from the all (cf. Eph. 1:4).

So the witnesses make the verse electing love: The witnesses note the delight in thy fathers: his special love on them. The witnesses note the to love them: of his own good pleasure. The witnesses note the chose their seed: above all people. The witnesses note the free grace: the God of all singling out the few (Eph. 1:4). The expositors observe the delight in thy fathers: his special love on them. The expositors observe the to love them: of his own good pleasure. The expositors observe the chose their seed: above all people. The expositors observe the free grace: the God of all singling out the few (Eph. 1:4). The believer is taught to mark the delight in thy fathers, the to love them, the chose their seed, the free grace, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that God, who owns all, delighted to love the fathers and chose their seed above all — the freeness of electing love, his grace singling out the few from the all, resting on his good pleasure alone (Eph. 1:4).
16:
"Circumcise therefore the foreskin of your heart, and be no more stiffnecked." The witnesses note the call to inward circumcision — of the heart, not the flesh only — and to cease from stubbornness. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were not to content themselves with, nor trust in, outward circumcision, but to be concerned for the circumcision of the heart — the removing of all carnality, sensuality, and hypocrisy. The witnesses note that they were to be no more stiffnecked. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the circumcise the heart — not the flesh only; the cut away the carnal — the corruption within; the be no more stiffnecked — ending stubborn resistance; the inward renewal — the circumcision of the heart by the Spirit (Rom. 2:29).

The witnesses agree the verse pierces beneath the outward sign to the inward reality God requires: not the circumcision of the flesh only but "of the heart" — the cutting away of all that is carnal and corrupt within, and the ending of their stubborn resistance to God; the demand for a circumcised heart is the demand for inward renewal, fulfilled only by the Spirit's work, "circumcision is that of the heart, in the spirit" (Rom. 2:29; Col. 2:11) — the true religion God seeks being a matter of the renewed and submissive heart, not the outward rite.

So the witnesses make the verse circumcise the heart: The witnesses note the circumcise the heart: not the flesh only. The witnesses note the cut away the carnal: the corruption within. The witnesses note the be no more stiffnecked: ending stubborn resistance. The witnesses note the inward renewal: the circumcision of the heart by the Spirit (Rom. 2:29). The expositors observe the circumcise the heart: not the flesh only. The expositors observe the cut away the carnal: the corruption within. The expositors observe the be no more stiffnecked: ending stubborn resistance. The expositors observe the inward renewal: the circumcision of the heart by the Spirit (Rom. 2:29). The believer is taught to mark the circumcise the heart, the cut away the carnal, the be no more stiffnecked, the inward renewal, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Israel was to circumcise the heart and be no more stiffnecked — the demand for inward renewal beneath the outward sign, fulfilled by the Spirit's circumcision of the heart (Rom. 2:29; Col. 2:11).
17:
"For the LORD your God is God of gods, and Lord of lords, a great God, a mighty, and a terrible, which regardeth not persons, nor taketh reward:" The witnesses note the majesty and impartial justice of God — God of gods, who shows no partiality and takes no bribe. The witnesses note that Gill notes he is God of gods — of angels and civil magistrates, so called — his creatures, accountable to him. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him a great, mighty, and terrible God, who regards not persons. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the God of gods — above all powers; the great, mighty, terrible — his supreme majesty; the regardeth not persons — no partiality; the nor taketh reward — no bribe, righteous to all (Acts 10:34).

The witnesses agree the verse joins God's supreme majesty to his perfect impartiality: he is "God of gods, and Lord of lords," above all powers in heaven and earth, yet "regardeth not persons, nor taketh reward" — the great and terrible God who judges without favour or bribe; the union of greatness and justice is at once awful and comforting, that the Almighty cannot be swayed by rank or riches but deals righteously with all, a terror to the proud oppressor and a refuge to the lowly, for with him is no respect of persons (cf. Acts 10:34).

So the witnesses make the verse God of gods: The witnesses note the God of gods: above all powers. The witnesses note the great, mighty, terrible: his supreme majesty. The witnesses note the regardeth not persons: no partiality. The witnesses note the nor taketh reward: no bribe, righteous to all (Acts 10:34). The expositors observe the God of gods: above all powers. The expositors observe the great, mighty, terrible: his supreme majesty. The expositors observe the regardeth not persons: no partiality. The expositors observe the nor taketh reward: no bribe, righteous to all (Acts 10:34). The believer is taught to mark the God of gods, the great, mighty, terrible, the regardeth not persons, the nor taketh reward, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that the LORD is God of gods, great and terrible, regarding no persons nor taking reward — his majesty joined to perfect impartiality, a terror to the proud and a refuge to the lowly (Acts 10:34).
18:
"He doth execute the judgment of the fatherless and widow, and loveth the stranger, in giving him food and raiment." The witnesses note God's care for the helpless — doing justice for the fatherless and widow, and loving the stranger. The witnesses note that Gill notes God is the patron and defender of the fatherless and widow, who have none to help them, doing them justice himself. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes his love to the stranger in giving food and raiment. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the judgment of the fatherless — justice for the defenceless; the and widow — their patron and defender; the loveth the stranger — the poor outsider; the giving food and raiment — the great God stooping to the needy (Ps. 68:5).

The witnesses agree the great and impartial God stoops in tender care to the weakest: he "doth execute the judgment of the fatherless and widow," and "loveth the stranger," providing the poor outsider food and raiment; the same majesty that owns the heavens bends to defend those who have no defender, showing that true greatness is joined to mercy, and setting the pattern his people must follow — that the God who needs nothing cares for the needy, a Father to the fatherless and a judge of the widow (cf. Ps. 68:5).

So the witnesses make the verse care for the helpless: The witnesses note the judgment of the fatherless: justice for the defenceless. The witnesses note the and widow: their patron and defender. The witnesses note the loveth the stranger: the poor outsider. The witnesses note the giving food and raiment: the great God stooping to the needy (Ps. 68:5). The expositors observe the judgment of the fatherless: justice for the defenceless. The expositors observe the and widow: their patron and defender. The expositors observe the loveth the stranger: the poor outsider. The expositors observe the giving food and raiment: the great God stooping to the needy (Ps. 68:5). The believer is taught to mark the judgment of the fatherless, the and widow, the loveth the stranger, the giving food and raiment, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that God does justice for the fatherless and widow and loves the stranger — the great and impartial God stooping in tender care to the weakest, a Father to the fatherless (Ps. 68:5).
19:
"Love ye therefore the stranger: for ye were strangers in the land of Egypt." The witnesses note the charge to love the stranger, drawn from God's love and Israel's own experience of being strangers. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were to love the stranger because the Lord loves him, and for the further reason that they themselves had been strangers. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the ground — for ye were strangers in Egypt. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the love the stranger — the charge of compassion; the because God loves him — the divine example; the ye were strangers — their own remembered estate; the fellow-feeling — softened toward others in like case (Exod. 23:9).

The witnesses agree the command to love the stranger rests on two motives — the example of God and the memory of their own estate: they were to love the stranger because God loves him, and because they themselves had been strangers in Egypt and known the stranger's hardship; the double argument teaches that the people of God are to imitate his compassion and to let their own past affliction soften them toward others in like case, doing to the sojourner as they would have been done by — love taught both by God's pattern and by fellow-feeling (cf. Exod. 23:9).

So the witnesses make the verse love the stranger: The witnesses note the love the stranger: the charge of compassion. The witnesses note the because God loves him: the divine example. The witnesses note the ye were strangers: their own remembered estate. The witnesses note the fellow-feeling: softened toward others in like case (Exod. 23:9). The expositors observe the love the stranger: the charge of compassion. The expositors observe the because God loves him: the divine example. The expositors observe the ye were strangers: their own remembered estate. The expositors observe the fellow-feeling: softened toward others in like case (Exod. 23:9). The believer is taught to mark the love the stranger, the because God loves him, the ye were strangers, the fellow-feeling, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Israel was to love the stranger, for they had been strangers in Egypt — love taught by God's example and their own remembered affliction, doing to the sojourner as they would be done by (Exod. 23:9).
20:
"Thou shalt fear the LORD thy God; him shalt thou serve, and to him shalt thou cleave, and swear by his name." The witnesses note the charge to fear, serve, cleave to, and swear by the LORD — a word Christ used against Satan. The witnesses note that Gill notes the fear of the Lord includes the whole worship of him, external and internal. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes that in the New Testament this was our Lord's last answer to the tempter — "thou shalt worship the Lord thy God, and him only shalt thou serve" (Matt. 4:10). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the fear the LORD — reverence him; the him shalt thou serve — service to him alone; the to him cleave — a clinging, unparted love; the swear by his name — allegiance to him only (Matt. 4:10).

The witnesses agree the verse gathers the whole of true devotion: to fear the LORD, serve him, cleave to him, and swear by his name only — reverence, service, attachment, and allegiance all directed to him alone; the added "cleave" presses a clinging love that will not be parted from God, and our Lord wielded the heart of this command against the tempter (Matt. 4:10), making it the believer's charter of undivided worship, that the one God is to be feared and served and held fast with the whole soul.

So the witnesses make the verse cleave to him: The witnesses note the fear the LORD: reverence him. The witnesses note the him shalt thou serve: service to him alone. The witnesses note the to him cleave: a clinging, unparted love. The witnesses note the swear by his name: allegiance to him only (Matt. 4:10). The expositors observe the fear the LORD: reverence him. The expositors observe the him shalt thou serve: service to him alone. The expositors observe the to him cleave: a clinging, unparted love. The expositors observe the swear by his name: allegiance to him only (Matt. 4:10). The believer is taught to mark the fear the LORD, the him shalt thou serve, the to him cleave, the swear by his name, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that Israel was to fear, serve, cleave to, and swear by the LORD alone — the whole of true devotion, a clinging love that will not be parted from God, Christ's answer to the tempter (Matt. 4:10).
21:
"He is thy praise, and he is thy God, that hath done for thee these great and terrible things, which thine eyes have seen." The witnesses note that God is his people's praise, who has done great and terrible things before their eyes. The witnesses note that Gill notes he is the object and matter of their praise, deserving the praises of all his creatures for his perfections, works, and blessings — praise is his due, and comely in his people to give. The witnesses note that he had done great and terrible things which their eyes had seen. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he is thy praise — the theme and ground of it; the he is thy God — the one to be praised; the great and terrible things — done before their eyes; the glory in him — the believer's boast and song (Jer. 9:24).

The witnesses agree the verse makes God himself the theme and ground of his people's praise: "he is thy praise" — both the one to be praised and the glory of those who praise him, for he has done great and terrible things before their very eyes; the seen wonders of his power and mercy are the matter of their thanksgiving, and the truth that God is the believer's "praise" lifts the heart to make him its boast and song, glorying not in self but in the God who has done such things for them (cf. Jer. 9:24, let him that glorieth glory in this, that he knoweth me).

So the witnesses make the verse he is thy praise: The witnesses note the he is thy praise: the theme and ground of it. The witnesses note the he is thy God: the one to be praised. The witnesses note the great and terrible things: done before their eyes. The witnesses note the glory in him: the believer's boast and song (Jer. 9:24). The expositors observe the he is thy praise: the theme and ground of it. The expositors observe the he is thy God: the one to be praised. The expositors observe the great and terrible things: done before their eyes. The expositors observe the glory in him: the believer's boast and song (Jer. 9:24). The believer is taught to mark the he is thy praise, the he is thy God, the great and terrible things, the glory in him, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that God is his people's praise, having done great things before their eyes — himself the theme and glory of their thanksgiving, the believer glorying not in self but in him (Jer. 9:24).
22:
"Thy fathers went down into Egypt with threescore and ten persons; and now the LORD thy God hath made thee as the stars of heaven for multitude." The witnesses note the increase from seventy souls to a multitude as the stars — God's faithfulness to his promise. The witnesses note that Gill notes the fathers went down with seventy persons in all, and now the Lord had made them as the stars of heaven for multitude, as he promised. The witnesses note that this fulfilled the promise made to Abraham. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the seventy persons — who went down to Egypt; the made thee a multitude — as the stars of heaven; the the promise kept — to Abraham fulfilled; the God faithful — his word performed (Gen. 15:5).

The witnesses agree the verse seals the chapter with a witness to God's faithfulness: from the seventy souls who went down into Egypt, God had multiplied Israel "as the stars of heaven," exactly as he had promised Abraham; the marvelous increase, set before their eyes, is the proof that God keeps his word, the very promise that made Abraham's seed as the stars now visibly fulfilled (Gen. 15:5) — a ground of confidence that the God who so multiplied them will perform all the rest of his promise, faithful from generation to generation.

So the witnesses make the verse as the stars: The witnesses note the seventy persons: who went down to Egypt. The witnesses note the made thee a multitude: as the stars of heaven. The witnesses note the the promise kept: to Abraham fulfilled. The witnesses note the God faithful: his word performed (Gen. 15:5). The expositors observe the seventy persons: who went down to Egypt. The expositors observe the made thee a multitude: as the stars of heaven. The expositors observe the the promise kept: to Abraham fulfilled. The expositors observe the God faithful: his word performed (Gen. 15:5). The believer is taught to mark the seventy persons, the made thee a multitude, the the promise kept, the God faithful, and to fear and love the LORD his God. The verse thus records that from seventy souls God had made Israel as the stars for multitude — his faithfulness to the promise made Abraham visibly fulfilled, a ground of confidence that he will perform all his word (Gen. 15:5).
